coredata.dc:title: 0D/2D heterojunction of graphene quantum dots/MXene nanosheets for boosted hydrogen evolution reaction
coredata.dc:creator: Xue, Ya
Xie, Yongshuai
Xu, Chenyu
He, Haiyan
Jiang, Quanguo
Ying, Guobing
Huang, Huajie
coredata.dc:description: Electrocatalytic hydrogen production is considered to be one of the cleanest hydrogen production pathways, while the development of advanced electrocatalysts with high activity and low cost is currently the biggest challenge in this field. Here, we report a robust and cost-effective approach to the construction of the 0D/2D heterojunctions made from small-sized graphene quantum dots strongly coupled with ultrathin Ti3C2Tx MXene nanosheets (GQDs/Ti3C2Tx) via a controllable assembly process. The newly-designed heterostructure provides unique structural advantages including large specific surface area, uniform dispersion of quantum dots with abundant active edge sites, desirable electronic structures, and good electronic conductivity, which endow the resulting GQDs/Ti3C2Tx catalyst with excellent hydrogen evolution properties with a low onset potential, a small Tafel slope, and long service life, which are significantly better than the bare quantum dots and Ti3C2Tx catalysts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
MXene
Heterojunction
Electrocatalyst
Hydrogen evolution reaction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration for the preparation of 0D/2D GQDs/Ti3C2Tx heterojunctions, which involves: (1) the production of Ti3C2Tx nanosheets from commercial Ti3AlC2 powders by a wet etching method; (2) the deposition of GQDs on Ti3C2Tx surfaces via a solvothermal assembly process.

Representative (a-b) FE-SEM, (c-d) TEM and (e-f) HR-TEM images of the 0D/2D GQDs/Ti3C2Tx heterojunction. (g) HAADF-STEM and the corresponding elemental mapping images of (h) C, (i) O, (j) Ti, and (k) F for the GQDs/Ti3C2Tx heterojunction.
(a) Typical XRD patterns of Ti3AlC2, Ti3C2Tx and GQDs/Ti3C2Tx. (b) Raman spectra of Ti3C2Tx, GOQDs and GQDs/Ti3C2Tx.
(a) XPS survey spectra of the GQDs/Ti3C2Tx and Ti3C2Tx samples. High-resolution (b) C 1s, (c) Ti 2p, and (d) O 1s XPS spectra of the GQDs/Ti3C2Tx heterojunction.

(a) Typical LSV curves and (b) Tafel plots of the GQDs/Ti3C2Tx with varying GQD contents, GOQDs, and Ti3C2Tx electrodes in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution. (c) The CV curves of the GQDs(5%)/Ti3C2Tx electrode recorded with potentials between 0.24 and 0.44 V (vs. RHE) at scan rates from 20 to 120 mV s−1 (d) The capacitive currents for the GQDs/Ti3C2Tx with varying GQD contents, Ti3C2Tx and GOQDs electrodes at 0.34 V plotted as a function of scan rates.

(a) The current-time curve of the selected GQDs(5%)/Ti3C2Tx electrode at −280 mV (vs. RHE). (b) Cyclic testing of the GQDs(5%)/Ti3C2Tx electrode. (c) Nyquist plots of the GQDs(5%)/Ti3C2Tx and reference GOQDs electrodes at corresponding open circuit potential with amplitude of 10 mV. (d) Local enlargement of the Nyquist plot for GQDs(5%)/Ti3C2Tx and the corresponding fitting curve. The inset in (d) is the equivalent circuit.

head.ce:title: 0D/2D heterojunction of graphene quantum dots/MXene nanosheets for boosted hydrogen evolution reaction

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Mechanics and Materials, Hohai University, Nanjing 210098, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Mechanics and Materials, Hohai University, Nanjing 210098, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Electrocatalytic hydrogen production is considered to be one of the cleanest hydrogen production pathways, while the development of advanced electrocatalysts with high activity and low cost is currently the biggest challenge in this field. Here, we report a robust and cost-effective approach to the construction of the 0D/2D heterojunctions made from small-sized graphene quantum dots strongly coupled with ultrathin Ti3C2Tx MXene nanosheets (GQDs/Ti3C2Tx) via a controllable assembly process. The newly-designed heterostructure provides unique structural advantages including large specific surface area, uniform dispersion of quantum dots with abundant active edge sites, desirable electronic structures, and good electronic conductivity, which endow the resulting GQDs/Ti3C2Tx catalyst with excellent hydrogen evolution properties with a low onset potential, a small Tafel slope, and long service life, which are significantly better than the bare quantum dots and Ti3C2Tx catalysts.

A facile and scalable strategy is developed to the construction of the 0D/2D graphene quantum dots/Ti3C2Tx MXene heterojunctions via a tunable assembly process. Benefiting from the remarkable synergetic coupling effects, the resulting heterojunctions show superior electrocatalytic performance for hydrogen evolution reaction.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:section.ce:para: Energy is the most important basis for human survival, daily life, and social development [1–4]. Facing the global energy crisis and environmental pollution, developing sustainable clean energy has become the unanimous choice of all countries in the world [5–8]. In the new round of energy reform, hydrogen (H2) has become the first choice for technologies of developing new energy carriers due to its advantages of high energy density, abundant sources, environmental friendliness, and zero pollution emission [9–11].

Over the past decades, water electrolysis has been regarded as a very potential hydrogen production technology [12,13]. However, the cathodic hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) involved in water electrolysis requires a high overpotential, which limits the production efficiency of hydrogen to a large extent [14,15]. In order to reduce the overpotential of water decomposition and improve the reaction rate, the development and utilization of high-performance HER electrode catalysts are regarded as an effective way, which has also become the key to the commercial application of electrochemical water splitting technology [16–18]. So far, precious metals (such as Pt, Ir, Ru, etc.) have been considered as the most effective HER catalysts, but their high cost and scarcity limit their large-scale use in actual hydrogen production [19,20]. Therefore, the development of low-cost electrocatalysts with high catalytic activity is the biggest challenge in the field of hydrogen production by electrolysis of water [21,22].

Two dimensional (2D) transition metal carbide/nitride (MXene), as the newest member of the 2D material family, not only has the similarity to graphene with natural large specific surface areas and ultrathin nanolamellar structure, but also has a series of additional structural advantages, including excellent metallic conductivity, tunable surface functional groups, good hydrophilicity, and reliable electrochemical stability [23–25]. Up to now, 2D Ti3C2Tx (Tx represents the terminal groups) nanosheet is the most intensively researched MXene material, which has been demonstrated as the active constituent for a variety of electrocatalytic applications [26,27]. Compared with traditional carbon nanomaterials, the Ti3C2Tx MXene nanosheets commonly possess more active surface chemical properties and thus provide abundant catalytic active sites [28–30]. In addition, the surface groups of Ti3C2Tx can be used as anchor sites to ensure the stable binding with nanoparticles when it is recombined with other catalytic materials [31,32]. Therefore, Ti3C2Tx MXene is expected to be a candidate material for the construction of the next-generation non-noble metal hydrogen evolution catalysts.

On the other hand, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are quasi-zero-dimensional (0D) nanomaterials with excellent quantum confined/edge effects and have recently become a hotspot in the fields of chemistry and materials [33–36]. In particular, GQDs do not possess the conventional layered structure and properties of graphene, but have a unique quasi-0D structure that endows them with abundant edge defect locations, which can provide a large number of active sites for electrocatalytic reactions [37–41]. Nonetheless, 0D GQDs with high surface free energy easily suffer from serious stacking or aggregation during the practical use, thus rendering a deteriorated catalytic ability [42,43]. Within this context, it is of great significant to design and construct a 0D/2D heterojunction of GQDs/MXene nanosheets, which may combine the respective structural merits of the components and thus achieve remarkable synergetic coupling effects. To our knowledge, neither fabrication nor catalytic performance of this interesting 0D/2D nanoarchitecture has been reported so far.

In this contribution, we present a robust and cost-effective method to the construction of the 0D/2D heterojunctions consisting of small-sized GQDs anchored onto ultrathin Ti3C2Tx MXene nanosheets (GQDs/Ti3C2Tx) through a controllable assembly process. As shown in Figs. 1 and S1-S4, ultrathin Ti3C2Tx nanosheets with an average thickness of about 3.0 nm were firstly prepared by the selective etching of the “Al” layers from commercial Ti3AlC2 powder with the assistance of lithium fluoride and hydrochloric acid based on our recent works [30,31]. Next, the as-prepared Ti3C2Tx nanosheets were dispersed in ethylene glycol solution by ultrasonic treatment, and then the dispersion was fully mixed with a certain amount of graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) solution with vigorous magnetic stirring (Fig. S5). During this process, the oxygen-containing species such as carboxyl and hydroxyl groups of GOQDs would easily connect with the functional groups on Ti3C2Tx surface, thus affording a stable 0D/2D heterostructure. Afterwards, the above mixture was sealed in an autoclave and solvothermally heated at 100 °C for 12 h, during which the abundant oxygenated functional groups of GOQDs would covalently bound to the surface of Ti3C2Tx nanosheets and simultaneously the conversion of GOQDs to GQDs could be achieved in the presence of ethylene glycol. Finally, the as-produced hybrid was freeze-dried to prevent the agglomeration of GQDs as well as the re-stacking of Ti3C2Tx nanosheets, giving rise to the desired 0D/2D GQDs/Ti3C2Tx heterojunctions. It is noteworthy that the loading amount of GQDs on the Ti3C2Tx nanosheets is conveniently tunable, and four different GQD contents of 1.0, 3.0, 5.0, and 10.0 wt% were chosen in this study, labeled as GQDs(1%)/Ti3C2Tx, GQDs(3%)/Ti3C2Tx, GQDs(5%)/Ti3C2Tx, and GQDs(10%)/Ti3C2Tx, respectively. Remarkably, the unique architectural design enables multiple catalytic active centers for the resultant GQDs/Ti3C2Tx heterojunctions: on the one hand, the large presence of structural defects on the edge sites of GQDs ensures sufficient active sites in the catalytic system; on the other hand, the Ti3C2Tx nanosheets with abundant functional groups is able to offer efficient hydrogen binding sites for water dissociation process. Working together, the optimized GQDs/Ti3C2Tx heterojunction manifests a much enhanced HER performance in comparison with the bare quantum dots and Ti3C2Tx catalysts.

Firstly, the 2D Ti3C2Tx nanosheets were obtained by etching commercial Ti3AlC2 powder (11 Technology CO., Ltd) with the help of LiF and HCl according to our previous studies [30,31]. 0D/2D GQDs/Ti3C2Tx heterojunctions with different GQD loading amounts (1.0∼10.0 wt%) were synthesized by a controllable solvothermal method. Taking the GQDs/Ti3C2Tx catalyst with the GQD content of 5.0 wt% as an example, the typical synthetic routes are as follows: 19 mg of fresh Ti3C2Tx nanosheets were suspended in 10 mL of ethylene glycol and 10 mL of deionized water to form a uniform colloidal solution. Subsequently, 1 mL of GOQDs solution (1 mg mL−1, XFNANO Materials Tech Co., Ltd) was added into above-mentioned Ti3C2Tx solution and vigorously stirred for 15 min to make them fully mixed. The resulting mixture was then transferred to a high-pressure reactor and reacted at 100 °C for 12 h. Afterwards, the reaction kettle was cooled naturally, and then the product was centrifuged and washed with deionized water for at least 3 times. After removing the impurity ions, the resulting sample, denoted as GQDs(5%)/Ti3C2Tx, was frozen completely by liquid nitrogen and finally dried in a freeze dryer for 24 h. Similarly, the GQDs(1%)/Ti3C2Tx, GQDs(3%)/Ti3C2Tx, and GQDs(10%)/Ti3C2Tx hybrids were also prepared by the same method with adjusting the feeding ratios of GOQDs to Ti3C2Tx.

The morphologies and microstructures of as-synthesized GQDs/Ti3C2Tx heterojunctions were systematically investigated by field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, JEOL JSM-6500F) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100) equipped with an X-ray energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS). Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD, a Bruker D8 ADVANCE diffractometer, Cu Kα radiation, λ≈ 1.54 Å), Raman microscope (Renishaw microscope with 532 nm laser) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, PHI Quantera spectrometer with Al Kα radiation, hν = 1486.6 eV) were exploited to study the crystalline and chemical properties of the as-prepared GQDs/Ti3C2Tx samples. The layer thickness for the Ti3C2Tx MXene was evaluated by atomic force microscopy (AFM, Nanoscope IIIA microscope).

The instrument used for electrocatalytic HER measurements is Shanghai Chenhua CHI 760E electrochemical station with standard three-electrode system. Among them, the glassy carbon (GC) electrode coated with catalyst is the working electrode, the saturated calomel electrode (SCE) is the reference electrode, and the graphite rod is the counter electrode. The preparation method of the working electrode was described in our previous studies [9]. The overall catalyst loading on the working electrode surface was about 0.142 mg cm−2. In the hydrogen evolution tests, the HER polarization curves of various electrodes were recorded by linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) in 0.5 M H2SO4 aqueous solution; the electrochemical active surface areas as well as long-term stability of the catalysts were investigated by cyclic voltammetry (CV); the current-time curves were collected by chronoamperometry; the charge transfer resistances of the catalysts were estimated through the alternating-current (AC) impedance method. The conversion equation used to calibrate SCE with respect to reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) is E(RHE) = E(SCE)+0.059pH+0.242.

The morphology and microstructure of the as-constructed 0D/2D GQDs/Ti3C2Tx heterojunctions were firstly studied by using FE-SEM and TEM. As shown in Fig. 2 a-b, the derived Ti3C2Tx nanosheets have an obvious 2D lamellar feature, which provides an ideal growth platform with plentiful binding sites for immobilizing tiny GQDs. At higher magnification (Fig. 2c-d), it is found that a large number of GQDs with an average size of only 5.3 nm were successfully loaded onto the both sides of the Ti3C2Tx nanosheets. Almost all GQDs are closely attached to the Ti3C2Tx matrix, indicating that there is a strong interaction between them. Notably, increasing the feeding ratios of GOQDs to Ti3C2Tx reveals that more and more GQD particles are anchored onto the Ti3C2Tx surface (Fig. S6), confirming our ability to regulate the composition of the hybrids. With the help of high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM), the detailed crystal structure of GQDs/Ti3C2Tx was further studied. As shown in Fig. 2e-f, it can be clearly seen that the crystal plane spacing of GQDs is measured to be 0.25 nm, corresponding to the (1120) plane of graphite carbon, while the interplanar distance of 0.23 nm is assigned to the (103) plane of Ti3C2Tx nanosheets. In addition, the high-angle circular dark-field scanning TEM (HAADF-STEM) and the corresponding elemental mapping images further unravel the elemental composition of the GQDs/Ti3C2Tx catalyst (Fig. 2g-k). As we expected, the GQDs/Ti3C2Tx catalyst is mainly composed of C, Ti, O, and F components, and these four elements are uniformly distributed on the entire nanosheet.

To gain more insights into the crystalline texture and chemical structure of the GQDs/Ti3C2Tx heterojunction, powder XRD and Raman spectroscopy tests were carried out. Fig. 3 a shows the typical XRD patterns of the GQDs/Ti3C2Tx, Ti3C2Tx, and Ti3AlC2 materials. Apparently, the (002) diffraction peak at around 2θ = ∼9.5° in the XRD pattern of bulk Ti3AlC2 powder shifts to a much lower angle of 2θ = ∼7.0° in the case of Ti3C2Tx, suggesting the successful exfoliation of few-layer Ti3C2Tx nanosheets. After the assembly of GQDs on the Ti3C2Tx layers, a prominent characteristic peak can be detected at about 2θ = 26.5°, which is induced by the (002) faces in the GQD structure [21]. It is worth noting that the characteristic diffraction peaks of Ti3C2Tx materials are almost invisible in the GQDs/Ti3C2Tx spectrum, which means that the incorporation of GQDs can effectively prevent Ti3C2Tx nanosheets from re-aggregating or re-stacking. In addition, the Raman spectra of both GQDs and GQDs/Ti3C2Tx (Fig. 3b) show two distinct Raman scattering peaks centered at 1372 and 1591 cm−1, corresponding to the D and G band of the graphitic material, respectively. At the same time, the characteristic peak centered at around 200 cm−1 is caused by the Ti atomic vibration of Ti3C2Tx nanosheets, which convincingly testifies the successful coupling of the GQDs and Ti3C2Tx nanosheets.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) technique was further used to analyze the surface element compositions and chemical states of the GQDs/Ti3C2Tx heterojunction. As can be seen from the XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 4 a), the C 1s, O 1s, and Ti 2p signals are clearly identified, which are consistent with the test result of EDS analysis (Fig. S7). Compared with the survey spectrum of Ti3C2Tx, the more pronounced C signal in the GQDs/Ti3C2Tx spectrum implies the efficient loading of GQDs on the Ti3C2Tx matrix. Meanwhile, the deconvoluted C 1s spectrum shown in Fig. 4b consists of five peaks located at binding energies of 282.4, 284.8, 286.0, 286.7, and 288.5 eV (Fig. 4b), which correspond to the C−Ti−Tx, C=C, C=O, CHx/C-O, and C=O groups, respectively [22]. In addition, the peak fitting of Ti 2p spectrum creates four different Ti signals (Fig. 4c) with binding energies of 455.3 (458.1), 458.6, 459.2, and 464.8 eV, indexed to the Ti−C, Ti−O−X, TiO2, and Ti-O2-xTx species, respectively [30]. Besides, the detected O element mainly arises from the surface oxygen-containing functional groups on the GQDs/Ti3C2Tx plane as well as its surface oxidation by air. The high-resolution O 1 s spectrum can be resolved into several peaks at 530.2, 531.7, and 533.2 eV (Fig. 4d), which are assigned to the Ti−O−X, HO-C=O and C=O configurations, respectively.

The electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution performance of the GQDs/Ti3C2Tx heterojunctions was systematically evaluated by various electrochemical techniques in an acid media. Fig. 5 a shows the LSV curves of the GQDs/Ti3C2Tx catalysts with different GQD contents in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution, accompanied with the bare GOQDs and Ti3C2Tx electrodes for comparison. Remarkably, it is found that the GQD loading amount has a notable influence on the HER activity of the GQDs/Ti3C2Tx electrodes. Specifically, when the GQD loading is 5 wt%, the lowest onset potential of ∼130 mV is achieved on the GQDs(5%)/Ti3C2Tx electrode, while the cathode current rises rapidly with increasing the negative potential. In addition, it can be seen that the GQDs(5%)/Ti3C2Tx electrode only needs a low overpotential of 260 mV to achieve a current density of 10 mA cm−2, followed by the GQDs(10%)/Ti3C2Tx, GQDs(3%)/Ti3C2Tx, and GQDs(1%)/Ti3C2Tx electrodes (Table S1), giving the highest HER activity. Moreover, Fig. 5b shows the corresponding Tafel plots of different catalysts. Among these electrodes, GQDs(5%)/Ti3C2Tx possesses the smallest Tafel slope of only 89 mV dec–1, proving again that the utilization of 0D/2D GQDs/Ti3C2Tx heterojunction with an appropriate GQD content is quite beneficial to promoting the HER kinetics. On the one hand, if the GQD content is too low (Fig. S6a-b), the number of catalytic active centers would be reduced, and simultaneously the synergistic effects with Ti3C2Tx nanosheets would be weakened, thereby reducing the HER performance of the GQDs/Ti3C2Tx heterojunction. On the other hand, excessive GQDs in the heterostructure are easy to cause serious re-agglomeration and re-stacking phenomenons (Fig. S6d), which would also deteriorate the catalytic ability of GQDs/Ti3C2Tx. Additionally, the reference GOQDs and Ti3C2Tx catalysts manifest very limited catalytic activity, which should be ascribed to their poor electronic conductivity or insufficient active sites.

The distinctly improved HER performance of the GQDs/Ti3C2Tx electrode demonstrates the structural advantages of the 0D/2D heterojunctions, which are expected to create much more active edge sites for hydrogen evolution. This speculation is further confirmed by the measurement of electrochemical double-layer capacitance (Cdl) to estimate the electrochemically active surface areas (ECSAs) for various catalysts. As displayed in Figs. 5c and S8, CV measurements were conducted in a potential range of 0.24 to 0.44 V, where the currents are mainly attributed to the charging of the double layer. Representative plots for the determination of active surface areas are shown in Fig. 5d. Among the different GQDs/Ti3C2Tx electrodes, GQDs(5%)/Ti3C2Tx possesses the largest average Cdl value of up to 11.2 mF cm−2, consistent with the LSV result. However, the Cdl values of Ti3C2Tx and GOQDs are calculated to be only 2.8 and 1.3 mF cm−2, respectively. Therefore, the ECSA is increased by 4∼8 times due to the strong coupling of nanosized GQDs with ultrathin Ti3C2Tx nanosheets, further evidencing that the optimized GQDs(5%)/Ti3C2Tx heterojunction can serve as a promising electrocatalyst towards the HER.

Besides the electrocatalytic activity and ECSA values, durability is another important performance index for the hydrogen evolution electrocatalysts in practical applications. In the present study, the time-dependent current density curve of the GQDs/Ti3C2Tx heterojunction was recorded by the chronoamperometric method to examine the long-term stability for 10,000 s (Fig. 6 a). Impressively, under a fixed electrode potential, the HER current density on the GQDs(5%)/Ti3C2Tx electrode was well maintained throughout the whole test, while the small fluctuation of the current is mainly due to the continuous generation and breakage of H2 bubbles on the electrode surface during the catalytic process, indicative of a reliable HER catalytic stability of the GQDs(5%)/Ti3C2Tx heterojunction. In order to further study the cycling performance of the GQDs/Ti3C2Tx heterojunction, continuous LSV measurements were also carried out. As depicted in Fig. 6b, the resultant LSV curve remained almost unchanged after 5000 cycles, evidencing that the GQDs(5%)/Ti3C2Tx catalyst is able to provide sustainable HER activity. FE-SEM analysis also confirms that the unique heterostructure of the GQDs(5%)/Ti3C2Tx catalyst can be preserved after the HER test (Fig. S9), which is ascribed to the stable interactions between GQDs and Ti3C2Tx substrate. Furthermore, the superior electron conductivity of the GQDs/Ti3C2Tx heterojunction may also contribute to its improved HER properties. As can be seen from Fig. 6c-d, the AC impedance spectra reveal that the as-derived semiarc for the GQDs/Ti3C2Tx electrode is much smaller than that of bare GOQDs electrode. According to an equivalent circuit, the charge-transfer resistance of GQDs/Ti3C2Tx is estimated to be only ∼4.1 Ω, in stark contrast to that of GOQDs electrode (174.7 Ω), which attests that the rational integration of GQDs and Ti3C2Tx nanosheets can effectively facilitate the electron transport in the HER process.

In summary, we have developed a robust and facile strategy to the construction of the 0D/2D GQDs/Ti3C2Tx MXene heterojunctions via a tunable assembly process. Such a unique architectural design not only enables a homogeneous dispersion of the ultrafine GQDs with abundant catalytically active edge sites, but also preserves the energetic surface properties and high metallic conductivity of the ultrathin Ti3C2Tx nanosheets. As a consequence, the resulting GQDs/Ti3C2Tx heterojunction with an appropriate GQD content (5 wt%) expresses a relatively low onset potential of 130 mV, a small Tafel slope of 89 mV dec−1, and excellent long-term durability towards the HER, far surpassing the bare GOQDs and Ti3C2Tx catalysts. It is believed that this synthetic protocol can be further extended to the bottom-up construction of other 0D/2D heterojunctions made from various quantum dots and MXenes, which is conducive to dealing with the challenges faced by the energy and environment fields.

Ya Xue: Investigation, Visualization, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Yongshuai Xie: Investigation, Software, Visualization. Chenyu Xu: Investigation. Haiyan He: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Quanguo Jiang: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Guobing Ying: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Supervision. Huajie Huang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Resources, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (11872171 and 51802077) and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (B210202093).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.surfin.2022.101907.
